summary: Jeff Atwood examines Charles Petzold's provocative essay questioning whether Visual Studio harms developers by imposing a rigid cognitive model on programming. While Petzold argues that IDE features like IntelliSense force bottom-up, linear coding and that code generation erects walls between programmers and their craft, Atwood offers a counterpoint. He notes that bad programmers write bad code regardless of tools, while RAD tools simply make good programmers more productive. Atwood ultimately disagrees with Petzold's nostalgic preference for Notepad coding, arguing that tighter IDE-language coupling represents the future of programming, not its corruption.
key_points: Petzold argues Visual Studio imposes a flawed cognitive model that degrades the programming experience, similar to how PowerPoint constrains presentations
IntelliSense encourages linear, bottom-up coding where variables must be defined before use, treating programmers like machines
Code generation in Visual Studio creates barriers between programmers and understanding, implying certain aspects of programming are only accessible through the IDE
RAD tools don't make good programmers write bad code - they simply let bad programmers write bad code faster while making good programmers more productive
The future likely lies in even tighter coupling between languages and IDEs, potentially making compilation outside the IDE impossible
money_quotes: Bad programmers write bad code. Good programmers write good code. RAD lets bad programmers write bad code faster.
It's not that IntelliSense is teaching us to program like a machine; it's just that IntelliSense would be much happier if we did.
As Visual Studio is generating code, it is also erecting walls between that code and the programmer.
It's just me and the code, and for awhile, I feel like a real programmer again.
Using Notepad to code may be an instructive exercise in minimalism for students, but no professional programmer can afford to build software this way.
A developer weaned on the Visual Studio .NET IDE is powerless outside that environment.
I think of it more as a scar I got after doing Windows programming for ten years.
key_insight: While IDEs may impose cognitive constraints on how we program, the productivity gains they provide make tighter tool-language integration inevitable, regardless of purist objections about programmer autonomy.
